title: Allow mounting volumes/host paths to a running container.
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

I'd like to be able to add and remove mounts on a running container. I have long-running container that I use for builds and tests of several related projects. I'd like to be able to mount and unmount the project source directories as I work on different parts of the system.

I think for my use case, being able to mount them during `podman exec` would be sufficient,

comments.body:
This is not possible via exec, but it might work with a volume mount from the host.

Theoretically you can mount a volume in the primary container as "slave", then  you can mount content onto the slave mount from the host, and it should show up in side of the container.

Here is a quick test I setup.
```
# mkdir /mnt/test
# podman run -d -v /mnt:/mnt:slave fedora sleep 10000
bb4dce265acf7dd67225563efca1d11183f9b85bcdac66d325366c59bc9ee397
# podman exec bb4dce265acf7dd67225563efca1d11183f9b85bcdac66d325366c59bc9ee397 ls  /mnt/test
# 
```
Notice there is nothing under /mnt/test,  Now I will create a directory with content and mount it on /mnt/test
```
# mkdir /root/dan
# touch /root/dan/walsh
# mount -o bind /root/dan /mnt/test
# podman exec bb4dce265acf7dd67225563efca1d11183f9b85bcdac66d325366c59bc9ee397 ls  /mnt/test
walsh
```
See the walsh file has now shown up in the container.
@giuseppe Do you think this is possible for rootless containers?
@rhatdan yes, that should work with rootless too.  As long as these operations are performed from the `podman unshare` environment
I was not able to get it to work in rootless mode.

> Theoretically you can mount a volume in the primary container as "slave", then you can mount content onto the slave mount from the host, and it should show up in side of the container.

I see how I could do this by hand, but I was hoping to have tools to manage it (in particular, wanting to avoid needing to manage a mountpoint manually. I am also doing this with rootless podman, which I guess might involve managing a mount namespace where the shared mount-point is created.

> This is not possible via exec, but it might work with a volume mount from the host.

My thought was that this could be slightly easier, in that exec-ed command could be run in a cloned mount namespace, where the additional mount was done, but maybe that isn't possible.

> I was hoping to have tools to manage it [...]

For the use case I have in mind, I'd be happy to explicitly annotate the mountpoint that I want to vary at `podman run` time.

podman exec enters the userns and mountns, you could do `podman exec--user root --privileged` but not sure you would have access to content on the host mount namespace at that time.

rootless containers can only do the following kinds of mounts

fuse, bind, procfs, overlay, sysfs, tmpfs (I believe).
